text: High load strength training (≥80% 1RM) improves running economy in middle- and long-distance runners with a small effect size.
Combined strength training methods (two or more methods) produce moderate improvements in running economy at speeds of 10.00 to 14.45 km/h.
Plyometric training improves running economy only at speeds ≤12.00 km/h, with a small effect size.
specific_value: ES = -0.307, p = 0.028, effective at speeds ≤12.00 km/h

text: Submaximal load strength training (40-79% 1RM) does not significantly improve running economy compared to endurance-only controls.
Isometric strength training does not significantly improve running economy compared to endurance-only controls.
High load strength training is more effective at improving running economy in athletes with higher VO2max values.
population: middle- and long-distance runners with high VO2max

text: High load strength training produces greater running economy improvements at higher running speeds.
Strength training programmes of 6 to 24 weeks duration with 1-4 sessions per week are sufficient to improve running economy when using appropriate methods (high load, plyometric, or combined).
